title: Kundalini Regression: When You Think You're Done and It Starts Again

body:
Kundalini Regression: When You Think You're Done and It Starts Again

You're here, aren't you? It's probably late. Maybe you can't sleep. You thought you were through the worst of it. You thought you'd finally put that whole wild, terrifying kundalini ride behind you. And now... it's back. Or something like it. And you're sitting there, heart pounding, stomach churning, wondering if you're losing your mind all over again. I know that feeling. I've been there. That sickening drop when you realize the peace you fought so hard for is slipping, or worse, completely gone. It's like a bad dream you can't wake up from, a nightmare you already lived through once. And now, the sequel no one asked for. It's not fair, right? You did the work. You survived. You adapted. You thought you'd earned your reprieve.

But here's the thing: kundalini isn't a one-and-done event for many of us. It's not a switch you flip and then it stays flipped. It's more like a river that can swell, recede, and then flood again, sometimes when you least expect it. And the regression, the feeling that you're going backward, that you're losing ground, that's almost as scary as the initial awakening. Because this time, you know what it can feel like. You know the depths of terror and confusion it can drag you into. And you're terrified of going there again. You're not alone in this. Not by a long shot. I've talked to countless people who've experienced this, and honestly, it's a common, if rarely discussed, part of the process for some.

The False Sense of Security

You probably reached a point where things felt stable, right? Maybe you even thought, "Okay, I've integrated this. I'm good now." You found some routines, some practices, some ways to cope. You started feeling like yourself again, or at least a new, more grounded version of yourself. You might have even started helping others, sharing your story, feeling like a survivor. That's a natural and healthy response to intense experiences. You build a new normal. You reconstruct your life around this new reality. And then, without warning, the floor drops out. It can be triggered by stress, by a new spiritual practice, by a traumatic event, or sometimes, by absolutely nothing you can pinpoint. It just... happens. The energy starts surging again, the old symptoms reappear - the buzzing, the heat, the involuntary movements, the intense emotional swings, the existential dread. It's like your system is being re-calibrated, but you didn't ask for the update, and it feels like a downgrade. You're left asking, "Why me again? What did I do wrong?"

You didn't do anything wrong. This isn't a punishment. It's just how this unpredictable energy works sometimes. It's got its own agenda, its own rhythm, and it doesn't always consult your carefully constructed peace. It's a rude awakening to the fact that you're still in process. That the work isn't finished. And that can be incredibly disheartening. It can make you feel like all your progress was an illusion. But it wasn't. You survived the first time. You learned. You grew. You're stronger now, even if you don't feel it in this moment of renewed chaos. You have tools you didn't have before. You have a frame of reference, however terrifying it may be. That's not nothing. That's everything, honestly.

What Does "Regression" Even Mean Here?

When I talk about regression in the context of kundalini, I'm not talking about going back to being an unaware, unawakened person. That's not how it works. Once your system has been activated, it's activated. What I mean by regression is the re-emergence of those intense, disorienting, and often distressing symptoms you thought you'd moved past. It's the feeling of losing your grip on reality, of being overwhelmed by energy, emotion, or strange physical sensations. It's the sense of being pulled back into the chaotic state you experienced during the initial crisis.

It can manifest in a few ways:

  • Physical symptoms: The burning, buzzing, vibrating, twitching, involuntary movements, digestive issues, extreme fatigue or insomnia, head pressure, nerve pain - all the fun stuff you thought was gone.
  • Emotional turmoil: Sudden, intense mood swings, anxiety that comes out of nowhere, bouts of depression, anger, grief, or fear that feel disproportionate to your current life circumstances. It's like your emotional thermostat is broken again.
  • Mental confusion: Difficulty concentrating, feeling spaced out, existential dread, questioning your sanity, vivid dreams or nightmares, intrusive thoughts, feeling disconnected from your body or the world.
  • Spiritual distress: Feeling abandoned by your guides or higher power, questioning your beliefs, feeling a deep sense of isolation, or even a return of psychic phenomena that you had learned to manage or thought had faded.

It's not truly a regression in the sense of undoing your progress, but it certainly feels that way. It's more like a deeper clearing, a second pass. Your system is saying, "Hey, we missed some stuff. Let's go again!" And you're stuck in the middle of it, wishing it would just stop. You know what I mean? It's like your inner house cleaner came back with a stronger bleach and decided to scrub out some more stubborn stains you didn't even know were there. And it's messy. It's always messy.

Why Does This Happen?

There's no single answer, and anyone who tells you there is, well, they're probably selling something. But based on my own experience and what I've seen in others, here are some possibilities:

Unfinished Business

Look, the initial kundalini awakening often clears out a lot of surface-level stuff - old traumas, limiting beliefs, emotional blockages. But sometimes, there's deeper, more ingrained material that needs to be processed. Think of it like peeling an onion. You get through a layer, you feel better, then another layer needs to come off. These deeper layers might be things you weren't ready to face the first time around, or they might be so deeply buried that they only become accessible once your system has been strengthened and refined. This "regression" might be the energy going back to address those root issues. It's unpleasant, but it's ultimately for your growth. It's like your soul is saying, "Okay, we're ready for the big stuff now."

Environmental Triggers and Stress

Life doesn't stop just because you've had a spiritual awakening. Stress, trauma, loss, major life changes - these can all act as triggers. If you're under immense pressure, your nervous system can become overloaded, and that can reactivate the kundalini energy in a way that feels chaotic and uncontrolled. It's like your system is trying to process the stress, but it's using the kundalini energy to do it, and that can feel like a resurgence of symptoms. Maintaining a calm, stable environment is hard enough without kundalini, right? Add that to the mix, and it's a recipe for feeling overwhelmed. So yeah, sometimes it's not even about "spiritual progress" but simply your body's way of dealing with intense external pressure.

Further Integration and Refinement

Sometimes, this isn't a regression at all, but a deeper integration. Your nervous system might be recalibrating to handle higher levels of energy, or your consciousness might be expanding further. This process isn't always smooth. It can feel like your brain is being rewired, your body is being upgraded, and your spirit is being stretched. This can be painful, disorienting, and frankly, terrifying. It's like a software update for your entire being, and sometimes those updates come with bugs and glitches before they settle. The Kundalini Experience by Lee Sannella (paid link) talks about these stages of activation, and how varied and unpredictable they can be. It's not always a linear path, you know?

Coping When It Comes Back

Okay, so it's back. What now? The good news - and there is good news - is that you've been through this before. You have a reference point. You have some tools, even if you feel like you've forgotten them in the panic. Here's what I've found helps, and what I've seen help others:

Grounding, Grounding, Grounding

This is your lifeline. When the energy feels like it's ripping you apart, you need to bring yourself back to earth. Physically. Mentally. Emotionally.

  • Nature: Get outside. Walk barefoot on the grass. Touch a tree. Feel the wind on your skin. Connect with something real and tangible.
  • Physical activity: Gentle exercise like walking, stretching, or yoga can help move the energy without overstimulating you. Avoid anything too intense if you're feeling overwhelmed.
  • Earthy foods: Root vegetables, protein, healthy fats. Avoid sugar, caffeine, and anything that makes you feel more jumpy. Your body needs solid fuel right now.
  • Sensory focus: Pay attention to your senses. What do you see, hear, smell, taste, touch? Anchor yourself in the present moment.

I can't stress this enough. When you feel like you're floating away or vibrating out of your skin, you need to deliberately bring yourself back into your body, into the here and now. It's not a cure, but it's a critical first aid measure.

Re-establishing Routine and Structure

Chaos thrives on lack of structure. When your inner world is in turmoil, your outer world needs to be as stable as possible. Stick to a schedule. Go to bed and wake up at the same time. Eat regular meals. Do your chores. Even if it feels meaningless, these small acts of normalcy can provide a sense of control and predictability when everything else feels out of control. It's like building a little fortress of sanity around yourself. Does that land? Because honestly, when I was at my worst, just getting out of bed and brushing my teeth felt like a victory, and it helped more than I thought it would.

Seeking Support (Again)

You probably had a support system the first time, or you learned how to find one. Don't be afraid to lean on it again. This is not a sign of weakness; it's a sign of intelligence. Talk to trusted friends or family members who understand (or at least try to understand) what you're going through. Reconnect with a therapist, especially one familiar with spiritual emergencies. When Spirit Leaps: Navigating the Process of Spiritual Awakening by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) is a fantastic resource for finding such support and understanding the process. There are communities online too, but be discerning. Not everyone out there has your best interests at heart, or even accurate information.

And yes, I'll say it again: if you're genuinely struggling, if you feel like you're losing your mind, or if you're having thoughts of self-harm, get professional help. This isn't something to "tough out" alone. There's no shame in needing help. None. Period. The Stormy Search for the Self by Stanislav & Christina Grof (paid link) goes deep into spiritual emergencies and how to get help. You're not the first, and you won't be the last, to need that kind of support.

Revisiting Your Practices - Carefully

If you had spiritual practices that helped you before, you might be tempted to jump right back into them. But be cautious. Sometimes, the very practices that helped you integrate before can become overstimulating when you're in a regression phase. Meditation, breathwork, or intense yoga might need to be modified or even temporarily paused. Listen to your body. Listen to your intuition. If a practice feels like it's making things worse, stop. It's not a failure; it's self-preservation. This isn't a time for pushing; it's a time for nurturing. Sometimes, the best "practice" is just radical self-care and rest.

Here's the thing: you're not back at square one. You're not starting over. This is a deeper dive. It's another layer. And it might be excruciating, but you have the resilience. You proved that already. This is just another test of that resilience, another opportunity for growth, however unwelcome it may feel right now. You've learned how to survive this. Now, you're learning how to move through it when you thought it was over. That's a different kind of strength, a deeper kind of wisdom. You'll get through this. You really will. Just keep breathing, keep grounding, and keep reaching out. You're not alone in the dark.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
